Exodus
  • Birth
  • Cause
  • Wars

What caused these wars?
Unrest in countries have primarily existed due to religious reasons, power struggles and resources.
But, there have been factors that accelerated these conflicts to devastating wars.
In turn, leaving millions homeless and on a terrifying journey, in search of new hope.

After World War II, there remained 2 powerful nations: US and USSR. USSR wanted to prevent Germany from invading Russia so they invaded eastern European countries, to create a communist-buffer between USSR and Germany.
US wanted a free-market oriented Europe and started expanding its markets in France and Japan. Soviets saw this growth of Capitalism as a threat to their Communist rule.
US and USSR developed immense nuclear arsenals and adopted a strategy deemed “MAD” i.e., Mutually Assured Destruction, with a capable of the extinction of our species. The cuban missile crisis was as close as possible that both nations came to the brink of a nuclear war. As a result, this situation had prevented direct conflicts between US and USSR and causing Proxy wars.After Korea and China became communist, US feared the domino-effect: that more & more countries would adopt communism.
 Vietnam  turning communist would be a threat to Japan, which US had helped turn into a Capitalist ally. Soviets assisted the North-Vietnamese communist army while US backed South-Vietnam during the Vietnam-war.
US executed several operations, in the Middle-east, to secure oil reserves. CIA engineered a coup to overthrow Iran’s democratically elected PM after he’d tried to nationalize Iran’s oil industry.USSR invaded  Afghanistan  and US backed the terrorist-group: Mujahideen, leading to the Soviet war in Afghanistan.
Eventually, Soviet Socialism did not prove to be a viable alternative to Industrial Capitalism as state-run economies did not fare well as private enterprises. Eventually, USSR liberalized its policies and more openness to the west. Communism started to collapse and finally resulted in the end of cold-war, in the 1980s.
Meanwhile, in the Middle-east, Shiites and Sunnis have had a power-struggle from long time. Saudi Arabia is prominently a Sunni-population while Iran is prominently Shia. Saudi Arabia forged an alliance with the US, owing to its massive oil-reserves.
Soon after, US staged a coup to transform Iran into a secular westernized country.However, this led to the Iranian-revolution, where the Iranians overthrew the Shah. Tensions arose, as the Iranian revolution terrified Saudi Arabia. What if their new leader would even inspire Saudi population to rise against the Saudi govt.
Iran began exporting its revolution by helping Shia groups, around the world, to overthrow governments in Iraq, Afghanistan and Saudi Arabia. Saudi-Arabia bolstered its alliance with US and other gulf monarchies to form the GCC.
 Iraq  under Saddam Hussein’s Sunni-regime invaded Iran, taking advantage of the Iranian revolution. When Iran started to win, Saudi Arabia came to Iraq’s support by providing money, weapons and logistical help. Millions died.
US invaded  Iraq  and overthrew Saddam Hussein. Neither Saudi Arabia and Iran did not want this to happen, as Iraq was a buffer between the 2 nations. Without a govt in  Iraq , armed militias took control. Shia and Sunni radical groups emerged. Sunni militia proxies were backed by Saudi Arabia while Shia militia proxies were backed by Iran.  Iraq  became the ground for a proxy war, with Iran and Saudi Arabia supporting opposite-sides.
Following the Arab-Spring, Saudi Arabia, the ultimate status-quo power did not want their people to revolt against the government while Iran was anti status-quo power and wanted to overthrow the regional-order, from ages.
These proxy wars have led to unpredictable situations, such as:
  • Tunisia: Saudi backs the dictator while Iran backs protestors.
  • Bahrain: Iran backs Shia-leaders while Saudi sends troops to the govt.
  • Yemen: Saudi military backs the govt. fight the Iranian proxy group, Houthi rebels
  •  Syria : Iranian military backs Hezbollah fight the Saudi-backed ISIS.
3. Rise of Terrorism
During the cold war, after the soviet invasion of  Afghanistan  , martyrs from everywhere flocked together to form the Mujahideen.
US had trained, armed and funded the Mujahideen to fight the Soviets. Mujahideen later split to form the Taliban and Al Qaeda. Once the war ended in 1989, Soviets withdrew and Al Qaeda grew into a global network. In 2001, Al Qaeda attacked the World trade center in US, claiming to avenge US’ actions, eventually leading to US invading Afghanistan.
The turning point occurred after US invaded  Iraq  and toppled Saddam’s secular dictatorship, Iraq was left unstable and Iraqi soldiers joined the insurgency. Al Qaeda, led by Osama bin laden merged with Abu Musab al-Zarqawi's Jama'at to form Al Qaeda in Iraq (AQI). AQI grew in large number and primarily attacked Shia, sparking a Shia-Sunni civil-war. However, US drone attacks severely weakened AQI.
In 2011, US withdrew from Iraq as it finally seemed stable. However, after Arab springs,  Syria’s  dictator cracked down violently on protestors, leading to a civil-war. Jihadists were released from prison against rebels.
AQI changed to Islamic State of Iraq (ISI), grew tremendously in number and expanded to Syria where it became Islamic State of Iraq and Syria (ISIS). There have been near By 2014, 1⁄3rd of Iraq was conquered by ISIS.
